old_claim.note: Military Waystations provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Roman Military Road Waystations provide the cursus-publicus relay-stage and official road-stopping context for military-administrative animal transport and communications.

source_shape.source_locator: Message-relay systems section says the cursus publicus used relay stages at intervals along Roman roads and that these stages were integral to the military and administrative system.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies Roman cursus-publicus relay stages as military-administrative road infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The veterinary node is scoped to late Roman cavalry, transport animals, and army supply systems; Roman road waystations fit that context, while Achaemenid Royal Road stations are a separate earlier node.

ontology_before: Military Veterinary Corps depended on Achaemenid military_waystations, conflating Persian royal-road stations with the late Roman military-administrative animal-care context.
ontology_after: Military Veterinary Corps depends on Roman Military Road Waystations for the Roman cursus-publicus relay-stage context, while Achaemenid military_waystations remain a separate Persian road-station node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: military_veterinary_corps now depends on military_road_waystations and no longer directly depends on military_waystations.
would_reject_if: The military_veterinary_corps node were rescoped to Achaemenid courier-horse care rather than late Roman horse medicine.
The military_road_waystations node were narrowed away from Roman cursus-publicus relay stages.
A stronger source showed Apsyrtos-era military veterinary practice specifically depended on Persian Royal Road institutions rather than Roman military-administrative road infrastructure.
short_reason: Late Roman veterinary support needs the Roman road-waystation node, not the Achaemenid one.
